schools.summary: Basil: he took our hunger, not from necessity but to provoke the conflict; the manhood truly tried.
Theophylact: tempted after baptism, to teach that temptation follows our baptism.
Ambrose: led to the wilderness to provoke the devil and to recall the exiled Adam; the Second Adam contends where the first fell.
Allusion to the Spirit's descent at baptism (Luke 3:21); sustained through the forty-day fast (cf Exod 34:28; 1 Kgs 19:8); Matthew's 'led' and Mark's 'driveth' agree.

outliers.note: Conjectures the Spirit caught Christ up bodily into the wilderness, as Philip was caught away (Acts 8:39).

schools.summary: Tried various ways through forty days, the three recorded temptations at their close (Matt 4:2); sustained by God yet truly hungering; Moses fasted thrice, Christ once.
Cyril of Alexandria: the eternal Word, equal to God in glory, stoops to hunger and to be tried in the wilderness.
'Son of God' anarthrous, the relationship not the office; the devil's 'if' assumes the Sonship (Luke 3:22); Luke's vivid singular 'this stone.'

themes: if thou be the Son of God

schools.summary: The answer from Deut 8:3 — the lesson Israel learned with the manna, that man lives not by bread alone but by God's word.
Gill: Jesus meets the tempter with 'It is written,' not with a display of power.
Theophylact: the second temptation moves from appetite to covetous desire of gain, all the kingdoms shown.
Ambrose: power is from God and not evil; the lust of power is from the evil one.
Luke's vivid 'in a moment of time' (stigme, only here) — a mental panorama; Luke's order differs from Matthew's but does not contradict (no 'then').

debates.question: What is the true order of the temptations?

sides.position: Matthew's order is the genuine one; Luke relates without strict sequence

schools.summary: Luke's specific claim of delegated sovereignty; Satan's real but usurped, permitted sway, the boast 'to whomsoever I will' a vaunt with no Scriptural foundation.
Wesley: 'Not so, Satan' — God, not the devil, sets up and puts down, though Satan by permission stirs revolutions.
Cyril: the riches are not the devil's; God says 'the gold is Mine, and to whomsoever I will I give it.'
Luke's delicate 'worship before me' (Matthew blunter), an ingressive aorist — bow the knee once; the condition subtly put.
Gill: a good angel refuses worship and points to God; a fallen one seeks it, even from the Son whom all holy angels worship.
Jesus turns the devil's own 'worship' against him from Deut 6:13 — one cannot serve both God and Satan; he must choose whom to serve.
Gill: spoken with indignation and detestation of the proposal.
Cyril: Christ, a Teacher of poverty, was never led to death for covetousness — the sinless One overcomes the bait of gain.
Ambrose: the weapon of boasting casts the boaster down; Augustine: the order differs from Matthew but all took place.
Origen: the willing wrestler, stronger in every trial; Athanasius: the tempter found no mark of his ancient seed in Christ and departed; Maximus on the later assaults through men.
The citation of Ps 91:11; Gill (after Jerome): Satan omits the middle clause 'to keep thee in all thy ways,' the words that expose his misuse.
'It is said' (perfect passive) from Deut 6:16 — each answer drawn from Deuteronomy; 'testing God is not trusting God' (Plummer); the misused promise rightly placed.
The three exhaust the avenues (appetite, nerves, ambition); 'tempted in all points' (Heb 4:15); 'for a season' implies later assaults — Gethsemane, the power of darkness (Luke 22:53; John 14:30).
Origen/Bede: power 'added' in its manifestation (miracles); Cyril: Christ works not by received grace like the saints but by his own power as the Son by nature.
Luke compresses, following Mark and leaving John's year of obscurity unfilled; a new Spirit-empowered phase, like Pentecost for the Apostles.
Clarke: he who overcomes temptation by grace is bettered by it; Christ returns in the Spirit's might.

themes: returned in the power of the Spirit

schools.summary: Imperfects: his habit of teaching, durative being-glorified; the synagogues open before Pharisaic hostility; the bright dawn of the ministry soon to meet opposition.
Calvin: from the first a divine power shone in Christ, compelling even the malignant to admire him.
Wesley: strong cordials after strong temptations — but neither the approval nor the calm lasted long.
Christ's regular synagogue attendance, an example even amid corruption; the rite (reading standing); peculiar to Luke; this visit before Mark 6:1, after a year's ministry.
Calvin: by expounding Isaiah and applying it he turned all eyes upon himself, keeping the synagogue's custom.
Tertullian (anti-Marcion): the name 'Nazarene' was foretold, marking the Christ of the Creator.
The synagogue rite: the chazan hands the roll; a lesson from the Prophets after the Law; the scroll unrolled; Isaiah formed a roll by itself.
Calvin: Christ deliberately chose this passage, a liberty of choice being allowed.
Wesley: he found it, opening upon it by God's particular providence.

debates.question: Did Jesus choose the Isaiah passage or light upon it?

schools.summary: Isa 61:1 (LXX, with Isa 42:7), applied by Christ to himself; 'anointed' (echrisen) — the Anointed One; the Spirit upon him as at the baptism; liberty alludes to the jubilee.
Calvin: Christ and his ministers act not by human authority but as sent by God, by the Spirit's guidance, that faith may rest on God's power.
Wesley: the Trinity interwoven; the gospel preached to the poor literally and spiritually.

themes: 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me'

schools.summary: The jubilee (Lev 25:9-10) — release, restoration, freedom — as type of the 'year' of the kingdom; Robertson rejects the one-year-ministry literalism of Clement/Origen.
Calvin: the time of redemption rests on God's good pleasure, 'in an acceptable time'; Wesley: the jubilee when debtors and servants were freed.
He rolled up the scroll, returned it to the minister, and sat down — the teaching posture; all eyes intently fixed (atenizontes), in calm deliberation.
Calvin: God touched their hearts with astonishment, holding them attentive so they did not interrupt.
Only the opening is preserved; 'hath been fulfilled' (perfect, 'stands fulfilled'), an arresting first sentence; what startled them was the carpenter's son claiming the prophet's word.
Calvin: he proved by facts that the time was come, applying Scripture to his hearers as a true expositor does.
Chrysostom: he refrains from miracles lest he provoke malice, but his teaching has ineffable grace softening hearers; Ambrose warns against grudging others' virtue.
Luke's 'words of grace' (absent from Matthew/Mark, later central in Paul); 'bare witness' inchoative — they began to own the rumours true.
Calvin: divine grace in Christ's lips, then the ingratitude of men — compelled to admire God speaking, yet refusing him.
The proverb 'Physician, heal thyself' = prove yourself to us; preserved by Luke the physician; they demanded the wonders of Capernaum on their own terms.
Calvin: Christ states the thoughts in their minds and imputes to their malice his declining to work miracles; Wesley voices their 'why not here?'
The proverb quoted often (Matt 13:57; Mark 6:4; John 4:44) — no prophet honoured at home; familiarity breeds contempt of the lowly neighbour.
Calvin: unbelief obstructs and Christ withholds his power as a rebuke (Mark 6:5); Wesley: the meanness of his family brings his office into contempt.
Two cases of prophets favouring foreigners; the 'three years and six months' (Jas 5:17; cf 1 Kgs 18:1) the popular reckoning of the drought, reconciled by counting from Sarepta.
Calvin: God may prefer strangers to the countrymen — an indirect rebuke of Nazareth's vanity and presumption.
Sarepta a Sidonian (Gentile) town (1 Kgs 17:9); the Greek marks contrast not exception — Elijah sent to a heathen widow, not to Israel's; a foreshadowing of grace to the Gentiles.
Naaman the Syrian, the lone leper cleansed by Elisha (2 Kgs 5), a heathen; 'but Naaman' marks contrast; the examples galled Nazareth as vilifying them like unbelieving Israel.
Clarke: God dispenses his benefits when, where, and to whom he pleases; none deserves them.
Cyril: enraged because he seemed to rank himself with the prophets; Ambrose: they lost salvation in casting out the Saviour.
They saw the point — God blessing the heathen over the Jews; the cases applied to themselves, he turned to others.
Calvin: they saw grace would pass from them to others and, instead of repenting, raged; Wesley: how changeable the hearts of the wicked.

A popular tumult, not lawful process (no Jewish power in capital matters); ingressive aorists of a move to lynch him at the brow (ophrys) of the hill; casting headlong no recognised Jewish punishment.

themes: thrust him out of the city

schools.summary: Calvin: an extraordinary miracle rescued him — God's power preserves us while he pleases; Gill: he showed his divine power.
Clarke/Wesley: he shut their eyes or overawed them — immortal till his time came (cf John 8:59).
Ellicott cautions against inserting a miracle: the moral power of self-possessed righteousness can baffle a mob; Robertson: 'he was going on his way.'

debates.question: Was the escape miraculous or the moral power of his presence?

sides.position: the moral majesty of self-possessed righteousness, no miracle assumed

themes: immortal till his work is done

schools.summary: Ambrose: not indignation but mercy moves him to teach the unbelieving; Bede/Cyril: his word is with power, joining miracles to teaching to rouse the unpersuaded.
Capernaum now the headquarters since Nazareth rejected him; parallel to Mark 1:21; Luke alone gives the reason for the removal.
Wesley: he dwelt at Capernaum, entirely quitting Nazareth.
His word 'with authority' (exousia, as Matt 7:29) — a new Lawgiver speaking in precepts, not citing the elders' traditions like the scribes; the Spirit's unction on all he said.
Luke's exact phrase 'a spirit of an unclean demon'; daimon never used in a good sense; the physician's precise language.
Calvin: by God's permission Satan had seized the man's faculties, the devils speaking and acting in and by him.
'Ah!' (Ea) a diabolical screech of fear and indignation; 'the Holy One of God' (cf Ps 16:10; Dan 9:24).
Wesley: the confession extorted by terror — the devils believe and tremble; the spirits know Christ better than unbelieving men.

themes: 'what have we to do with thee?'

schools.summary: Luke's exact verbs: the spirit hurled him down (rhipsan) yet 'did him no hurt' (blapsan, a medical word, here and Mark 16:18); Jesus rebuked the spirit, not the man, silencing and expelling it.
They spake one to another; the ambiguous 'for/that'; the miracle fastens attention on the word as the seat of authority and power.
Calvin: they are compelled to own something more than man in Christ, tracing the power to his doctrine which the devils obey.
'Fame' (echos, a sound — our 'echo'); the imperfect 'kept going forth,' a vivid picture of the resounding influence of the day's work; the first recorded exorcism.

Ambrose: having healed a man, he heals a woman — come to heal each sex; Bede: he cures at others' request or his own, granting the faithful's prayer; Chrysostom: he honoured his disciples by dwelling among them.
The house of Simon and Andrew (Mark 1:29); Luke the physician notes a 'great fever.'
Clarke: Peter's family benefited as soon as he followed Christ — one faithful, praying person draws blessings on a household.
'He stood over her' (only in Luke), a physician's attitude, and 'rebuked' the fever; the instant, complete cure (she ministered at once) proves a power nature cannot match.
Calvin: diseases are God's heralds, sent and restrained at his command; Christ rebukes the fever as its sovereign.
At sunset the sabbath ended (reckoned sunset to sunset), when it was held lawful to carry the sick (John 5:10); a vivid sunset scene as the news spread.
Theophylact/Origen: the multitude's zeal, bringing their sick after sunset, held back only by the sabbath scruple.
Imperfect 'came out' (one by one); 'Thou art the Son of God' a more definite confession of deity (cf Luke 3:22); he silenced them, refusing testimony from demons; read 'demons,' not 'devils.'
Cyril: the Jews denied Christ, but the devils confessed him.

themes: devils confess the Son of God
he suffered them not to speak

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having bestowed favour by miracles he departs (miracles thought greater when the worker is gone); he prayed in the desert as our example, not from need.
Harmonising Mark 1:35: withdrawal before day for prayer after the sabbath's excitement; the people 'earnestly sought' and would detain him.

'I must' — the urge to evangelise all the cities, not a favoured few; 'for therefore was I sent' (Johannine ring), Christ the Apostle of God; preaching the kingdom his great work, healings the secondary proof.
Periphrastic imperfect 'was preaching' — his first Galilean tour in accord with the stated purpose, details from Mark 1:39; through the synagogues of all Galilee.
